rationale: Redis uses a single-threaded event loop (redis-server) for request handling, with I/O threads (io_thd_*) assisting in reading/writing client data. Background I/O threads (bio_close_file, bio_aof, bio_lazy_free) handle deferred operations like file closing, AOF fsync, and lazy memory freeing. Forked child processes handle RDB snapshots (redis-rdb-bgsave) and AOF rewrites (redis-aof-rewrite). These background operations are CPU and I/O intensive and compete with the main event loop and I/O threads for CPU time. Deprioritizing background threads ensures the latency-critical event loop and I/O threads maintain low response latency.

threads.purpose: Background I/O thread for deferred file close operations. Handles closing file descriptors asynchronously to avoid blocking the main event loop.
Background I/O thread for AOF (Append Only File) fsync operations. Performs periodic fsync of the AOF log to disk.
Background I/O thread for lazy memory freeing. Deallocates large objects asynchronously to avoid blocking the event loop.
Forked child process for RDB snapshot persistence. Serializes the entire dataset to disk. CPU and memory intensive due to copy-on-write.
Forked child process for AOF rewrite. Compacts the append-only file by rewriting it with the current dataset state.
Main event loop thread. Handles all client connections, command parsing, and execution. Single-threaded request processing — any scheduling delay directly increases request latency.
I/O helper threads for multi-threaded I/O (Redis 6+). Handle client read/write operations in parallel with the main event loop. Latency-critical as they directly serve client requests.
